coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic copper-cerium nanoclusters: Assembly-induced aggregation into nanowire network and cysteine-triggered strong red fluorescence turn-on for highly sensitive and selective cysteine sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Yuanyuan
Wang, Jin
Liu, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: Supramolecular self-assemblies enabled to obtain architectures with amusing properties for various applications including optical device, biosensing and bioimaging. Herein, bimetallic copper-cerium nanoclusters (Cu/Ce NCs) spheres dispersed in isopropanol (IPA)/phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH7.0) mixed solution can be aggregated to form non-fluorescent nanowire network through self-assembly. Interestingly, the non-fluorescent Cu/Ce NCs nanowire network can be further aggregated blocky structure in the presence of cysteine (Cys), with a strong red fluorescence turn-on. Moreover, the Cys-induced turn-on efficiency of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% (v/v) IPA/PB mixed solution is 2.7-times that value in PB solution. The Cys-triggered red fluorescence turn-on of Cu/Ce NCs presumably due to the solvent-caused aggregation-induced emission, given rise to by the strong coordination interaction of Ce with carboxyl groups of Cys, and Cu with Cys via the thiol group to link with Cu/Ce NCs by CuS bonding. Additionally, it was found that the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution exhibited good storage stability and repeatability. Then, based on Cys-triggered red fluorescence turning on, fluorimetric sensing Cys was developed with a wide linear range of 10–32,000 nM and detection limit of 3 nM (S/N = 3). The other common species didn’t affect Cys detection. Furthermore, the as-proposed assay can be applied to measure Cys in actual samples accurately and reproducibly. Thereafter, featured by the solvent-induced self-assembly of metal NCs, which is envisioned to cast broad potential applications in designing luminescent materials, biosensing, clinical diagnosis and optoelectronic device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic copper-cerium nanoclusters
Supramolecular self-assemblies
Aggregation induced emission
Fluorescence turn-on
Cys sensing

Experimental section
Materials and reagents
Apparatus
Synthesis and characterization of Cu/Ce NCs
To characterize Cys-triggered fluorescence turn-on
Selectivity test of the as-developed method
Calibration curve and fluorimetric analysis of Cys in real samples
Results and discussion
Solvent-induced Cu/Ce NCs to self-assemble to nanowire network
Cys-triggered Cu/Ce NCs red fluorescence remarkably turning-on in IPA/PB mixed solution and possible mechanism
Optimization of conditions for enhancing the fluorimetric turn-on efficiencies for sensing Cys
Optimization of Cu/Ce NCs synthetic conditions
Effect of IPA ratio in the mixed solution
Optimization of conditions for enhancing the fluorimetric turn-on efficiencies for sensing Cys
Calibration curve for Cys
The selectivity of the as-proposed Cys sensor
Measurement of Cys in actual samples
Stability and repeatability of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution to detect Cys
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgements
Supplementary material
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and (B) the size distribution of Cu/Ce NCs in aqueous solution, (C, D) Cu/Ce NCs prepared from 60% IPA/PB mixed solution.

Fluorescence spectra of Cu/Ce NCs aqueous solution (a), Cu/Ce NCs aqueous solution + 1 µM Cys (b), Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution (c), Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution + 1 µM Cys (d) and 1 µM Cys prepared with 10 mM PB buffer (pH7.0) (e). Insets, photographs of solutions irradiated with UV light. Cu/Ce NCs, 5.0 mM.
TEM images of (A) Cu/Ce NCs aqueous solution+ 1 µM Cys and (B) Cu/Ce NCs prepared from 60% IPA/PB mixed solution + 1 µM Cys.

The UV–visible spectra of Cu/Ce NCs aqueous solution (a), Cu/Ce NCs aqueous solution + 1 µM Cys (b), Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution (c), Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution + 1 µM Cys (d) and 20 mM GSH (e).
(A) Fluorimetric spectra of Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM) as a function of Cys concentration. Inset, the solution pictures under UV light irradiation. (B) Working curve for Cys.

Selectivity test. (A) The fluorescence turning-on efficiencies of Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM) in 60% IPA/PB solution with Cys (1.0 µM) and different AAs and GSH (each 100 µM). (B) The fluorescence turning on efficiencies of Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM) in 60% IPA/PB solution with Cys (1.0 µM) and other ions including various ions (each 10 µM). Insets, corresponding photos under the UV light.
Schematic red fluorescence turn-on triggered by Cys along with the formation of blocky structure for Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution.
Analysis of Cys in real samples.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic copper-cerium nanoclusters: Assembly-induced aggregation into nanowire network and cysteine-triggered strong red fluorescence turn-on for highly sensitive and selective cysteine sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences, and School of Pharmacy, Medical College, Qingdao University, 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences, and School of Pharmacy, Medical College, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences, and School of Pharmacy, Medical College, Qingdao University, 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Institute for Food and Drug Control, 7 Longde Road, Qingdao 266073, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao Institute for Food and Drug Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingdao Institute for Food and Drug Control, 7 Longde Road, Qingdao 266073, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supramolecular self-assemblies enabled to obtain architectures with amusing properties for various applications including optical device, biosensing and bioimaging. Herein, bimetallic copper-cerium nanoclusters (Cu/Ce NCs) spheres dispersed in isopropanol (IPA)/phosphate buffer (PB, 10 mM, pH7.0) mixed solution can be aggregated to form non-fluorescent nanowire network through self-assembly. Interestingly, the non-fluorescent Cu/Ce NCs nanowire network can be further aggregated blocky structure in the presence of cysteine (Cys), with a strong red fluorescence turn-on. Moreover, the Cys-induced turn-on efficiency of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% (v/v) IPA/PB mixed solution is 2.7-times that value in PB solution. The Cys-triggered red fluorescence turn-on of Cu/Ce NCs presumably due to the solvent-caused aggregation-induced emission, given rise to by the strong coordination interaction of Ce with carboxyl groups of Cys, and Cu with Cys via the thiol group to link with Cu/Ce NCs by CuS bonding. Additionally, it was found that the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution exhibited good storage stability and repeatability. Then, based on Cys-triggered red fluorescence turning on, fluorimetric sensing Cys was developed with a wide linear range of 10–32,000 nM and detection limit of 3 nM (S/N = 3). The other common species didn’t affect Cys detection. Furthermore, the as-proposed assay can be applied to measure Cys in actual samples accurately and reproducibly. Thereafter, featured by the solvent-induced self-assembly of metal NCs, which is envisioned to cast broad potential applications in designing luminescent materials, biosensing, clinical diagnosis and optoelectronic device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique characteristic of aggregation induced emission (AIE) and its high quantum yield, it holds significant importance to design new optical materials and apply in optoelectronic devices and sensing [1]. The most important finding is that non-luminescent oligomeric nanoclusters (NCs) complexes can produce very potent luminescence upon aggregation [2]. For instance, Xie’s group designed highly luminescent gold-thiolate NCs [3]. The controlled aggregation of gold(I)-thiolate complexes on the gold(0) cores generated in-situ to form gold(0)@gold(I)-thiolate core−shell NCs with strong luminescence [3]. This self-assembly usually occurs through van der Waals forces, electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding or ππ stacking. The ordered aggregates can be mainly controlled by changing the temperature, metal ion coordination and solution pH [4–6]. The self-assembly induced NCs with improved luminescence, which is usually accompanied by emission spectral shifts. What’s noteworthy is that metal NCs can be induced to self-assemble [3]. Moreover, to study the directed self-assembly of AIE and metal NCs is more advanced to improve its enhanced emission characteristics with high controllability for luminescent metal NCs. Metal NCs has the merits of high sensitivity, high accuracy, as well as rapidness and in-expensiveness, and has a wide range of applications [7–15]. In addition, it is well known that fluorimetric biosensors depend to a large extent on the optical and physio-chemical properties [10,16–22]. Moreover, compared with single metal NCs, the bimetallic NCs have great synergistic [23] and antagonistic effects [24] in fields of biosensing, bioimaging and tumor therapy [25–32]. For instance, our group found that bimetallic Au/Cu NCs exhibited no fluorescence, nevertheless, red fluorescence turning-on can be strongly triggered with histidine (His), and accordingly, sensitive His sensor was explored [33].

Cysteine (Cys), as a kind of abundant biological thiol in biological system, where it performs diverse biochemical functions in metabolism, eliminates phlegm, protects liver cells and detoxification [34–36]. Cys is also regarded a potential neurotoxin, and its levels may be highly correlated with many diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease, liver damage, hair discoloration and slow growth [7,9,37]. Therefore, it is crucial to detect Cys level for their early diagnosis. The traditional methods for Cys include biochemical analyzer, capillary electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography [38–40], however, most of them have some disadvantages, for instance, time-consuming procedure, expensive instrumentation and tedious sample pretreatment that limit their point-of-care application. Therefore, there is intense interest to develop a simple, cost-effective, fast and miniaturizable Cys sensor for clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: Copper sulfate, cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate, and other inorganic salts were obtained from TCI Development Co. Ltd. IPA was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. GSH, human serum albumin (HSA) and amino acids (AA) including Cys, asparagine (Asn), Leucine (Leu), methionine (Met), serine (Ser), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), throsine (Thr), trptophan (Trp), alanine (Ala), valine (Val), arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Try), His and glutamic acid (Glu) were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of nanomaterials were performed on a JSM-2100 Plus (JEC, Japan). The UV–vis spectra were conducted using a U-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The ζ-potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement of the solutions were recorded by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, U.K.). The fluorescence spectra were performed with F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cu/Ce NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Ce(NO3)3 solution (5.0 mM, 5.0 mL) was mixed with CuSO4 solution (10 mM, 5.0 mL) under strong stirring for 10 min. Then, GSH aqueous solution (5.0 mL, 10 mM) was syringed dropwise into above solution under stirring at room temperature. After that, 0.70 mL NaOH solution (1 M) was introduced into the mixture and reacted under stirring for 5 h at room temperature. Finally, the resultant solution was dialyzed against water (The cut-off molecular weight of the dialysis film is 3500).

ce:section.ce:section-title: To characterize Cys-triggered fluorescence turn-on
ce:section.ce:para: The Cys-triggered fluorimetric turn-on efficiency was studied. Fluorimetric turn-on efficiency is defined as (F-F0)/F0 × 100%, where F0 and F are meant the fluorescence intensities of the Cu/Ce NCs without or with Cys (λex = 380 nm, λem = 650 nm), respectively. The fluorescent spectra of Cu/Ce NCs induced by Cys in PB solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) or 60% (v/v) IPA/PB mixed solution were compared. The detection conditions for Cys were optimized by varying Cu/Ce NCs concentrations, solution pH, temperature and response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test of the as-developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM) were dispersed in 60% IPA/PB solution (pH 7.0). Then, the fluorescence intensities were measured in Cys (1 µM) and varying AA, GSH, and common ions (Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, NO3 -, I-, SO4 2-, S2-, HPO4 2- and Cl-), separately. Their respective fluorimetric turn-on efficiencies were calculated for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and fluorimetric analysis of Cys in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorimetric assays for Cys were conducted with Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB solution (pH 7.0) by the following procedure. Cys solution with different concentrations were separately added into Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM) in 60% IPA/PB solution (pH 7.0) and stirred for 3 min. Then fluorescence intensity was measured. The working curve was drawn by plotting the fluorimetric turning-on efficiencies with Cys concentration.

The actual sample detection was carried out by standard addition method under the optimized experimental conditions. Human serums samples were obtained by the affiliated hospital of Qingdao University by collecting from healthy volunteers with informed consent, which were coagulated by the protein precipitation route for 2 h at 37 °C and kept overnight in a refrigerator at 4 °C. After the serums were naturally separated, centrifugation was conducted at 3000 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Solvent-induced Cu/Ce NCs to self-assemble to nanowire network
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu/Ce NCs were prepared by the biominerization step using GSH as template. GSH (isoelectric point of 2.85) is negatively-charged at pH 7.0, which makes Cu/Ce NCs showing excellent water-solubility [41]. Cu/Ce NCs in aqueous solution showed homogeneous and monodisperse spheres (2.0 ± 0.35 nm in diameter) by TEM images ( Fig. 1A), characteristic of metal NCs with average diameter of 2 nm (Fig. 1B). Interestingly, when Cu/Ce NCs were dispersed in the 60% IPA/PB mixed solution, the aggregates of spherical NCs formed nanowire network structure through self-assembly (Fig. 1C, D). The solvent-induced self-assembly can degrade the stability of Cu/Ce NC in water (dielectric constant about 80.1) with a weak polar solvent IPA (dielectric constant ~ 17.9) [42]. Changes in solvent polarity may lead nanoclusters to assemble tightly, which further promotes form long-range nanowire, limits the rotational and/or vibrational motion of individual nanoclusters [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cys-triggered Cu/Ce NCs red fluorescence remarkably turning-on in IPA/PB mixed solution and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu/Ce NCs in aqueous solution (curve a, Fig. 2) and Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA (curve c, Fig. 2) displayed negligible fluorescence. Cys has no fluorescence, either (curve e, Fig. 2). Surprisingly, when Cys was added to the Cu/Ce NCs dispersion, remarkably strong red fluorescence presented (curve b and d, Fig. 2), which was considered to be Cys-triggered turning on red fluorescence. More importantly, the enhanced fluorescence intensity of Cu/Ce NCs + Cys in IPA/PB mixed solution is 2.7-fold that value in aqueous solution. Obviously, the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution can exacerbate the fluorescence turn-on triggered by Cys. This color change can also be seen from the pictures of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution with or without Cys (inset, Fig. 2). By contrast, after the addition of IPA and Cys to the Cu NCs, Ce NCs or their mixture, either of them didn’t exhibit any fluorescence enhancement (Fig. S1). Therefore, the Cu/Ce NCs that we synthesized was not simple mixture of Cu NCs and Ce NCs.

To make clear the possible mechanism, TEM images of the aggregates were measured. The presence of Cys caused the Cu/Ce NCs nanospheres (Fig. 1A) to aggregate into small blocky structure ( Fig. 3A). Further, in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution, Cys induced the Cu/Ce NCs nanowire network (Fig. 1B, C) aggregated large blocks (Fig. 3B). Therefore, the fluorescence emission of Cu/Ce NCs was significantly enhanced by the aggregated state based on AIE effect. To investigate this, the UV–vis spectra of the above different mixtures were measured. The GSH-templated Cu/Ce NCs exhibited four peaks at 250 nm, 300 nm, 330 nm and 360 nm (curve a, Fig. 4). The peak at 250 nm was derived from GSH (curve e, Fig. 4) and the peak at 300 nm from Ce NCs (curve a, Fig. S2), while the peaks at 330 nm and 360 nm were rooted in Cu NCs (curve b, Fig. S2). Nevertheless, after adding Cys into Cu/Ce NCs solution, the peaks at 300 nm, 330 nm and 360 nm of Cu/Ce NCs were disappeared (curve b, Fig. 4), further indicating that there are interactions between Cys and Cu or Ce. To verify this, the ζ-potentials of Cu/Ce NCs in different cases were investigated (Table S1). In the absence of Cys, the ζ-potentials of the Cu/Ce NCs in aqueous solution and in IPA/PB mixed solution were − 5.6 mV and − 20.99 mV, respectively, while after the addition of Cys, their ζ-potentials were lowered to − 20.1 mV and − 36.3 mV, respectively, accordingly. The decreased ζ-potential indicates that the Cys molecules were bound to Cu/Ce NCs and Cu/Ce NCs nanowires, respectively [44]. The GSH protective layer of Cu/Ce nanowires also provides steric hindrance to make the distinction between Cys and GSH possible, considering that the molecular weight of Cys (~ 121 Da) is less than that of GSH (307 Da). Thus, the thiol ligand (Cys) can penetrate the possible GSH protecting layer of Cu/Ce NCs to interact with Cu, Ce atoms [45]. Actually, Ce has strong coordination interaction with carboxyl group of Cys [46], and Cu can interact with Cys through the thiol group to link with Cu/Ce NCs by Cu−S bonding. Thus, when Cys was added into Cu/Ce NCs in IPA/PB solution, the Cu/Ce NCs nanowire networks will be further crosslinking each other, finally to form large blocky structure (Fig. 3B), which change would result in significant fluorescence to turn-on. This aggregation phenomenon can also be verified by DLS measurements (Fig. 1B, Fig. S3). Taken together, the solvent polarity-caused assembly induced Cu/Ce NCs to aggregate to nanowire network, which was further triggered by Cys to exhibit strong red fluorescence turning-on along with the formation of blocky structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for enhancing the fluorimetric turn-on efficiencies for sensing Cys
Optimization of Cu/Ce NCs synthetic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the synthesis conditions of Cu/Ce NCs on the fluorescence turning on efficiency was studied. The highest Cys-triggered turning-on efficiency was obtained for the Cu/Ce NCs synthesized with the Cu-to-Ce ratio of 2:1 (Fig. S4A) and GSH dosage of 20.0 mM (Fig. S4B). Thus, the Cu/Ce NCs used in the following experiments were prepared with Cu-to-Ce ratio of 2/1 and GSH dosage of 20.0 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of IPA ratio in the mixed solution
ce:section.ce:para: The volume ratio of IPA and PB solution (v/v) in the mixed solution on fluorescence turn-on efficiency was studied. It was found that the Cys-triggered turn-on efficiency was highest when containing 60% IPA in the mixture solution (Fig. S5). It should be noted here, the mixed solution of IPA with 10 mM PBS was still homogeneous, which will not affect the subsequent application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for enhancing the fluorimetric turn-on efficiencies for sensing Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions for Cys detection were optimized by varying Cu/Ce NCs dosage, solution pH, temperature and response time. One can see that the Cu/Ce NCs dosage of 5.0 mM could result in the strongest fluorimetric turning on efficiencies when adding 1 µM Cys (Fig. S6A). Then, the fluorescence responses of Cu/Ce NCs to Cys in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution were explored by changing solution pH values. Clearly, the best pH for Cys sensing was pH 7.0 (Fig. S6B). On the other hand, the incubation temperature was optimized. Obviously, the turn-on efficiency was greatest at temperature of 25 °C (Fig. S6C). Finally, the incubation time of Cu/Ce NCs with Cys in 60% IPA/PB solution was examined. In 60% IPA/PB mixed solution, the fluorescence intensity of Cu/Ce NCs increased sharply by adding Cys, and the fluorescence intensity became stable in 3 min (curve a, Fig. S6D). Yet, Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution could present faster Cys response than in PB only (Fig. S6D). Taken together, the Cys sensing conditions are: Cu/Ce NCs (5.0 mM in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution), pH 7.0, temperature (25 °C), and response time (3 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve for Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution was employed to detect Cys. As the concentration of Cys increased, the fluorescence intensity of Cu/Ce NCs increased ( Fig. 5A). The calibration curve was plotted as the fluorescence turn-on efficiency changing with the logarithm of Cys concentration (Fig. 5B). The linear range was from 10 nM to 32,000 nM (R2 = 0.9938). The limit of detection (LOD) was approximated as 3 nM (S/N = 3). The performances of the as-proposed fluorimetric sensor and other methods are listed in Table S2. Apparently, our fluorimetric sensor is excelled in both wide dynamic range and lower LOD. For instance, the LOD for Cys was 100 nM based on colorimetry using AuNPs as the sensing element [47], 50 nM for complex-V2O5 nanosheet [48], 20 nM for fluorescent Ag NCs [49], and 120 nM for poly(methacrylic acid)-based fluorescent probe [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the as-proposed Cys sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing selectivity of fluorometric turn-on of Cu/Ce NCs to Cys in 60% IPA/PB solution was explored by testing the turn-on efficiency of Cys and with other AA and different ions. It was discovered that only Cys could trigger the turning on fluorescence in IPA/PB solution, whereas other AA (100 times excess) and different ions (10 times excess) exhibited no fluorescence change ( Fig. 6). As we addressed in Section 3.3.2, the Cys-triggered specifically fluorescence turning on Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution, which allows to sensitively detect Cys in complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Cys in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the standard addition method, the possibility of the as-developed method for Cys in actual samples (human serums) was evaluated. The detected Cys concentration matched well with the known concentration measured by biochemical analyzer. Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSD) were within 2.34%, and their recoveries were ranging within 96.8–103.6% ( Table 1), demonstrating that the as-developed analytical method has great reproducibility and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and repeatability of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution to detect Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The long-term stability of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution was explored in comparison with Cu/Ce NCs in PB solution. After storage in the darkness for three months, for the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution, 98% of the turn-on efficiency was remained. The turn-on efficiency only decreased by 4.2% after storage for 6 months (Fig. S7A), while the Cu/Ce NCs decreased by 68% after six months. The optical stability for Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA and Cu/Ce NCs was examined by irradiating under Xenon lamp at 8-time intervals. Considering that prolonged light exposure can cause photobleaching, we investigated the effect of exposure time on Cu/Ce NCs [51]. Apparently, Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution displayed insignificant change on turn-on efficiency even irradiated by strong light up to 14 h (curve a, Fig. S7B), contrastingly, Cu/Ce NCs had an obvious decline (curve b, Fig. S7B). Further, the ionic intensity has no effect on the fluorimetric turn-on efficiency of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution (curve a, Fig. S7C) and Cu/Ce NCs (curve b, Fig. S7C). Finally, the repeatability of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution and Cu/Ce NCs in PB buffer for detection of Cys was investigated. The turn-on efficiency of 7 different batches of NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution and Cu/Ce NCs in PB buffer retained essentially at the same level (Fig. S7D). These data indicate that the as-proposed fluorescent assay with Cu/Ce NCs in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution is stable for Cys sensing. Additionally, the stability of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution was better than in PB solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we found that bimetallic Cu/Ce NCs spheres dispersed in the mixture of IPA and water can be self-assembled to form network of nanowires. More excitingly, when Cys was added, the non-fluorescent Cu/Ce NCs nanowire network formed large blocky structure, with a strong red fluorescence turn-on. The Cys-induced turn-on efficiency of Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in 60% IPA/PB mixed solution is 2.7-times that value in PB buffer. The possible mechanism for the Cys-triggered red fluorescence turn-on of Cu/Ce NCs is that when Cys was added into Cu/Ce NCs in IPA/PB solution, the Cu/Ce NCs nanowire networks was further crosslinked each other, finally to form large blocky structure, which change would result in significant fluorescence to turn-on. Additionally, it was found that the Cu/Ce NCs dispersed in IPA/PB mixed solution could not only achieve high turn-on efficiency, but also improved environmental and storage stability. Then, based on Cys-triggered red fluorescence turn-on, fluorimetric Cys sensing was explored with linear range of 10–32,000 nM and LOD of 3 nM. Moreover, this sensor is capable of detecting Cys in human serum samples accurately and reproducibly. Thus, featured by the advantages of solvent-induced self-assembly strategy, which is prospected to find potential applications in biosensing, clinical diagnosis and optical display devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Yuanyuan Cai: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jin Wang: Investigation. Aihua Liu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174081).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supporting information
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131356.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary material
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stimuli-responsive properties of aggregation-induced-emission compounds containing a 9,10-distyrylanthracene moiety

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.R. Jia, H.J. Yu, J. Chen, W.J. Gao, J.K. Fang, Y.S. Qin, X.K. Hu, G. Shao, Stimuli-responsive properties of aggregation-induced-emission compounds containing a 9,10-distyrylanthracene moiety, Chem-Eur J, 24(2018) 19053–19059.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation-induced emission in luminescent metal nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, Q. Yao, S.Q. Zang, J. Xie, Aggregation-induced emission in luminescent metal nanoclusters, Nat. Sci. Rev., 5(2020) 15–19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From aggregation-induced emission of Au(i)–thiolate complexes to ultrabright Au(0)@Au(i)–thiolate core–shell nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Luo, X. Yuan, Y. Yu, Q. Zhang, D.T. Leong, J.Y. Lee, J. Xie, From aggregation-induced emission of Au(i)–thiolate complexes to ultrabright Au(0)@Au(i)–thiolate core–shell nanoclusters, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 134(2012) 16662–16670.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of nanoclusters into mono-, few-, and multilayered sheets via dipole-induced asymmetric van der waals attraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, J. Liu, Y. Li, Z. Cheng, T. Li, H. Zhang, Z. Lu, B. Yang, Self-assembly of nanoclusters into mono-, few-, and multilayered sheets via dipole-induced asymmetric van der waals attraction, ACS Nano, 9(2015) 6315–6323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent metal nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Goswami, Q. Yao, Z. Luo, J. Li, T. Chen, J. Xie, Luminescent metal nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission, J. Phys. Chem. Lett., 7(2016) 962–975.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lead phthalocyanine as a selective carrier for preparation of a cysteine-selective electrode

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuanyuan Cai is currently a Ph.D. student at Qingdao University under supervision of Prof. Aihua Liu. Her research interests are synthesis of metal nanoclusters and their biosensing application.

Aihua Liu received his Ph.D. degree from Tohoku University, Japan (2004) with major in biosensing. Then he worked in the National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science & Technology, Japan under the Japanese Society for the Promotion of Sciences (JSPS) fellowship (2004–2006). After that he moved to the US and conducted his postdoc research in Michigan State University, University of Oklahoma and University of Texas at Arlington. He was appointed to the Professor of Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy & Bioprocess, Chinese Academy of Sciences, where he led the Laboratory of Biosensing (2010–2016). In 2016, Dr Liu was appointed as Professor and Director of Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, Qingdao University. His research interests cover nanozyme, biotemplated synthesis, microbial surface display and their applications in biosensors and nanomedicine.
